label: Causation
scenario_patterns: A patient dies after a misdiagnosis, but the underlying condition was already terminal
Two defendants independently commit tortious acts, each sufficient to cause the same harm
A plaintiff's pre-existing condition is aggravated by the defendant's negligence
An intervening event occurs between the defendant's breach and the plaintiff's injury
legal_tests: But-for test: but for the defendant's breach, would the plaintiff have suffered the damage? If the damage would have occurred regardless, causation fails
Material contribution test: where the but-for test is unworkable (e.g., multiple tortfeasors), the plaintiff may show the defendant's breach materially contributed to the harm
Novus actus interveniens: an intervening act may break the chain of causation if it is not reasonably foreseeable
Remoteness: the type of damage must be reasonably foreseeable even if the exact manner or extent need not be (Wagon Mound as applied in SG)
Loss of chance: in some contexts, courts assess whether the defendant's breach caused the plaintiff to lose a real or substantial chance of a better outcome
common_fact_patterns: Medical negligence where causation is complicated by the patient's underlying health conditions
Road traffic accidents with multiple contributing vehicles or causes
Industrial disease claims with multiple potential sources of exposure
Professional negligence where the plaintiff must show the advice, if correct, would have led to a different outcome
Failure to warn cases where the plaintiff must prove they would have acted differently if warned
sg_case_references: Man Mohan Singh s/o Jothirambal Singh v Zurich Insurance (Singapore) Pte Ltd [2008] SGCA 24 — but-for causation and assessment of damages
JSI Shipping (S) Pte Ltd v Teofoongwonglcloong [2007] SGCA 40 — causation in the context of solicitor's negligence
Sunny Metal & Engineering Pte Ltd v Ng Khim Ming Eric [2007] SGCA 36 — causation and remoteness of damage
Armstrong, Carol Ann v Quest Laboratories Pte Ltd [2020] SGCA 25 — loss of chance in medical negligence
party_roles: plaintiff/claimant bearing the burden of proving causation
defences: The damage would have occurred in any event (but-for test not satisfied)
Novus actus interveniens — an unforeseeable intervening act broke the chain of causation
The type of damage suffered was not reasonably foreseeable (remoteness)
Pre-existing condition — the plaintiff's injury was not caused or materially worsened by the breach
